Grace. Space. Pace. And something in the pocket.

The Gentleman Villain

How the Jaguar Mark II became Britain's most beautiful getaway car.

By CarveyanceJaguar Mark II · 1959–19672026

The Jaguar Mark II should have been respectable.

Look at it quickly and you see the usual ingredients of old British dignity: walnut veneer, leather chairs, chrome trim, thin pillars, proper proportions, and the faint suggestion that the driver owns either a country jacket or a questionable tax arrangement.

But look again.

The stance is too tense. The haunches are too alive. The glasshouse is too light. The grille is not smiling. It is waiting.

That is the Mark II's genius. It looked like a gentleman's saloon and behaved like something that knew where the back roads were.

This was not simply an elegant four-door Jaguar. It was one of the original fast luxury saloons: a car with room for four, leather and wood inside, disc brakes all round, and, in 3.8-litre form, enough straight-six muscle to run with genuine sports cars. The 3.8 made a claimed 220 bhp, could reach 125 mph, and was always the one to have. Jaguar built 83,701 Mark IIs between 1959 and 1967 — 27,848 of them the coveted 3.8.

In the early 1960s, that was not normal.

That was borderline rude.

It was also why the Mark II became one of the most fascinating cars in British history. Executives wanted it. Enthusiasts revered it. Police forces used it. Racers drove it. And — inconveniently for Jaguar's brochure department — bank robbers loved it. The Mark II lived on both sides of the law, which may be why it still feels so alive today.

The Bank Robber's Jaguar

Sir William Lyons almost certainly did not set out to build Britain's finest getaway car. That would have been a difficult line to place in the brochure.

“New Jaguar Mark II: leather, walnut, disc brakes — and ideal for leaving the scene rapidly.”

But that is exactly what happened. The Mark II became bound up with Britain's 1960s underworld because it offered the criminal classes something they needed very badly: speed with manners. When the gang behind the 1963 Great Train Robbery needed wheels, a Mark II was among them. Of course it was.

A bank robber did not need romance. He needed doors.

A sports car might be glamorous, but it is useless when four men in coats need to get in quickly, one of them is panicking, another is carrying a bag, and someone in the back is shouting that the police have arrived. The Mark II had four doors, a useful boot, serious acceleration, and a shape that did not instantly announce trouble. It looked like a doctor's car, a solicitor's car, an executive's reward. Parked outside a bank, it could appear completely legitimate.

Until it wasn't.

The larger-engined cars were genuinely quick. Period testers put the 3.8 among the world's best saloons and marvelled at the value: 125 mph, real quarter-mile pace, four-wheel disc brakes, all for the money. That is the heart of it. The Mark II was not merely stylish. It was useful. It could sit outside a hotel looking polished and proper, cruise through town like a respectable businessman's car, and then, if required, become a weapon. Not a vulgar weapon. Not a hot rod. Something worse: a socially acceptable one.

It was a fast car that could pass as a normal one. That made it perfect for the underworld.

Why Criminals Chose It

The Mark II offered four things a getaway driver wanted.

First, speed. A genuine 120-plus-mph saloon in early-1960s Britain was a serious thing. Most ordinary cars of the period were slow, narrow, softly sprung and mechanically overwhelmed by hard, high-speed work. The Jaguar was different. It could surge, cruise, brake, and keep going. This was not a car that could do 100 mph once, downhill, with a prayer. This was a car built by a company with Le Mans in its bloodstream.

Second, space. The Mark II had room for people and luggage — which, in the wrong circles, meant men, tools, cash, masks, and other awkward items not commonly found in the official Jaguar accessory catalogue.

Third, brakes. This matters more than people realise. Speed gets you away; brakes keep you alive. Four-wheel Dunlop discs were advanced for the era, and they hauled the Mark II down better than almost anything it was up against.

Fourth, camouflage. This may have been its greatest advantage. A Mark II was not a thug's car by appearance. It was not painted with flames. It did not sit there coughing through side pipes like an escaped dragster. It looked respectable. That was the trick — it was not the car a witness remembered as suspicious until it was already disappearing.

The Mark II was a gentleman's car that criminals could borrow for ungentlemanly work. Very British, really.

The Police Wanted Them Too

The delicious irony is that the police used Mark IIs as well.

Of course they did. What else were they going to chase Mark IIs with — a sternly worded Austin? The Mk2 became the patrol car of the 1960s precisely because it was fast, nimble and British-built, and forces up and down the country pressed it into traffic duty. (It also became, years later, the car of a certain claret-coloured, opera-loving detective — though Inspector Morse, in a nice twist, drove the slowest 2.4.)

This is where the legend becomes perfect. The Mark II was not simply the robber's car. It was the copper's car too. It became both the chase and the chased.

Imagine the scene. A wet A-road. Sodium lamps. A Jaguar ahead, another Jaguar behind. One full of men who would prefer not to stop. One full of men professionally committed to making them. Same grille. Same haunches. Same straight-six thunder. Britain pursuing itself in stereo.

Few cars get to live on both sides of the law like that. The Mark II did, and it looked magnificent doing it. That is why the folklore stuck. It was not just fast. It was dramatic — cinematic before cinema knew what to do with it. A Rover could look official. A Mercedes could look important. A Jaguar Mark II looked like it knew a secret.

How It Drove: The Beautiful Threat

To understand the Mark II, you have to forget the modern idea of performance.

Today, a fast saloon is usually an electronic agreement between horsepower, software, traction control, adaptive dampers, tyre technology and a legal department. You press the throttle and a computer decides how much courage you are allowed to have.

The Mark II is different. The Mark II is mechanical speed.

You open the door and step into a room, not a cockpit. The cabin is leather, walnut, chrome, glass and ceremony. The pillars are slim. The dashboard is formal. The instruments are proper little circles with proper little needles. The whole thing smells of hide, varnish, old carpet, petrol and memory. It does not wrap around you like a modern sports saloon. It receives you.

Then you start it. The XK straight-six does not bark like a V8 or buzz like a small Italian engine. It has a cultivated growl — turbine smoothness with a metallic edge. At idle, it sounds expensive. Under load, it sounds educated, but not harmless.

The clutch is deliberate. The gearbox asks for respect. Early cars used the Moss manual, which is not something you flick through casually while thinking about lunch — you select a gear, you pause, you place it. Later all-synchromesh cars are far easier, and overdrive turns high-speed cruising into something genuinely relaxed. Then you lean into the throttle, and the car changes character.

The cabin says clubroom.
The engine says Le Mans cousin.

The 3.8 does not need thrashing to feel special. It has torque — 240 lb-ft at 3,000 rpm — and torque is what makes an old performance car feel alive in modern traffic. It does not explode forward like a super-saloon; it surges. The bonnet lifts slightly, the six clears its throat, and the car moves with that wonderful old sensation of mass being persuaded by engineering rather than bullied by electronics.

The steering is heavy at low speed, especially without assistance. Around town, you work for it — parking a Mark II unassisted must have given certain getaway drivers the forearms of a dockworker. But once you are moving, it makes sense. It is slower than a modern rack, yes, but it has weight and honesty. You do not so much aim the car as guide it.

On a flowing road, the Mark II has rhythm. Brake early. Turn with smooth hands. Let the front settle. Feel the rear axle follow. Feed in the throttle. Let the six pull you out. Do it right and it feels magnificent. Do it badly and the car will write you a strongly worded letter through the rear tyres.

Because it was never perfectly balanced. Independent front suspension, but a live rear axle, and a tail that could come alive — especially in the wet. Drive one hard on a fast, bumpy road decades later and the pace still astonishes, even as that live axle and those narrow tyres make themselves known over the bigger bumps.

That is the edge. That is the magic. The Mark II is not dangerous because it is bad. It is dangerous because it is good enough to tempt you. A slow car keeps you honest through lack of opportunity. A modern fast car keeps you honest through computers. A Mark II keeps you honest through consequences.

Why It Was So Special

The Mark II was special because it got the recipe right before the recipe had a name.

Today we understand the fast luxury saloon. BMW M5. Mercedes-AMG E-Class. Cadillac CT5-V Blackwing. Alfa Romeo Giulia Quadrifoglio. Four doors, serious power, rear-drive attitude, long-distance ability, a luxury cabin, and enough performance to embarrass a purpose-built sports car. These cars make sense to us now.

The Mark II was doing that in 1959. It was, in the truest sense, the original sports saloon: a four-door with the power and poise to keep up with sports cars, plus luxury in abundance. That is why the car matters.

It was not a warmed-over family saloon. It was not just a luxury car with a bigger engine. It was a complete idea — grace, space and pace, delivered in one compact, gorgeous, slightly dangerous package.

And it looked right, which matters. Some cars have performance. Some cars have presence. The Mark II had both, and the tension between the two is what makes it unforgettable. It looked formal enough for a bank manager and fast enough to worry the bank's security manager. The thin pillars gave the cabin air and light. The curved glass made it feel modern. The chrome was tasteful rather than desperate. The rear haunches gave it muscle without vulgarity. It was compact but not small, elegant but not weak, pretty but not soft. It did not shout about performance. It whispered it, with menace.

Was It Revered? Yes — But Not Innocently

The Mark II was absolutely revered, but not in a clean, museum-label way.

It was revered by road testers, who placed the 3.8 among the world's best saloons and praised not just its speed but the sense of well-being created by all that leather, veneer and refinement. It was revered by buyers — more than 83,000 of them — making it one of Jaguar's great commercial successes of the era. It was revered by racers: this was a car that could wear number roundels on Sunday and still take someone to dinner on Monday, and it duly bullied the British Saloon Car Championship, reeling off twenty wins on the trot between 1961 and 1963. For a while, it was simply the fastest four-door saloon money could buy.

It was revered by the police, who used it because it could actually do the job. And yes, it was revered by criminals — which is not exactly the endorsement Jaguar's marketing department would have requested.

But that is why the car became immortal. The Mark II was not just admired. It was useful. It moved people, chased people, escaped people, thrilled people and frightened people. A Ferrari becomes legendary because children put it on bedroom walls. A Jaguar Mark II becomes legendary because both the police and the criminals wanted the keys.

Against the Germans: Mercedes-Benz 220SE

Put the Jaguar beside a Mercedes-Benz 220SE and the difference becomes philosophical.

The Mercedes was the proper answer. Strongly built, refined, beautifully engineered, conservative in the best German sense — the car for someone who believed the world should be orderly and that a door ought to close with the sound of a bank vault agreeing with you.

The Jaguar was the improper answer. Faster. Sexier. More dramatic. Less predictable. Much more likely to make you take the long way home.

The Mercedes had deep quality. The Jaguar had quality of experience. The Mercedes made you feel sensible. The Jaguar made you feel alive. This is not to insult the Mercedes — quite the opposite. The 220SE was dignified, beautifully made and deeply respectable. But next to the Mark II it felt like a statesman beside a racing driver who had somehow been allowed into the dining room. If your priority was durability, composure and engineering conservatism, the Mercedes made sense. If your priority was speed, beauty and emotional voltage, the Jaguar had already left.

Against Rover: The Establishment Meets the Escape Artist

The Rover P5 was dignified. Prime-ministerial. Clubby. Deeply respectable. A car that looked as though it had been designed around the phrase “steady on.”

The Jaguar was clubby too — but the club had a back exit.

The Rover was for being driven to Parliament. The Jaguar was for leaving Parliament quickly, possibly before questions. As luxury transport, the Rover had enormous charm: dignity, comfort, old-world confidence. But as an object of desire, the Jaguar had danger — and danger, when wrapped in walnut and leather, ages extremely well. The Rover made you want to sit in the back. The Jaguar made you want to drive.

Against BMW and Alfa: The Future Arrives

This is where the Mark II becomes most interesting. Against Mercedes and Rover, the Jaguar looks rebellious. Against BMW's Neue Klasse and Alfa Romeo's Giulia, it starts to look more traditional.

Line a Mk2 up against a BMW 1800 and an Alfa Giulia and the Jaguar answers with a twin-cam six and four-wheel disc brakes — but the two smaller cars are plainly the fresher, more modern thought. The BMW was the future beginning to organise itself: cleaner, lighter, more rational, less ornate. You can see the seeds of the 3 Series and 5 Series in it. The Alfa was the sporting poet — lighter, sharper, fizzier, more eager.

The Jaguar was neither of those things exactly. It was older in atmosphere but advanced in concept. Old-world materials, new-world performance. Leather and picnic-table manners, but disc brakes and a twin-cam straight-six. It was both past and future in the same body.

That is part of the beauty and the tragedy of the Mark II. It was ahead and behind at the same time. Its performance predicted the super-saloon future; its styling belonged to an older, more romantic Britain. It was not purely modern.
It was not purely old-fashioned.
It was the bridge.

The Jaguar Awakening

I am not sure when it happened.

Maybe it was not one moment. Maybe it was a slow leak in the old bias system. Maybe some buried part of me finally stopped listening to the jokes about Lucas electrics, oil leaks, fragile reputations, and 1980s XJ6s parked outside mechanics' shops with their bonnets up like surrendered flags.

But somewhere along the way, I woke up to Jaguar. And once you wake up to Jaguar, it is very difficult to go back to sleep.

It is like finally reading a classic book you always knew existed. You had heard the title. You knew a few famous lines. You had seen the cover on shelves for years. Then one day you actually open it, and suddenly you understand why people have been talking about it for generations.

That is what Jaguar can do. It is not simply a car company. At its best, it is a language — a little English, a little engineering, a little theatre, a little trouble.

For years I knew the obvious chapters: XK120, XK140, C-Type, D-Type, E-Type, XKSS. I knew the headlines. I knew the Le Mans mythology — the long bonnets, the wire wheels, the racing green, the leather straps, the slightly heroic smell of fuel and old money. My father spoke of Jaguar's racing heritage when I was young, but somehow I did not see the cars that way. To me, Jaguar had become something else: charming but fragile, pretty but unreliable, elegant but faintly old — the sort of thing owned by retired men who said “motoring” instead of “driving.”

Then the cracks started forming. There was the XJR in the late 1990s, tail out in a magazine photograph, rear tyres turning into white smoke, its stately body doing something no stately body had any business doing. There were the XK8 and XKR, cars I admired but did not yet fully understand. Then, years later, that Steve McQueen photograph with the XKSS — the angle, the man, the car, the whole thing landing like a perfectly composed argument.

Then came the road sighting: a mint XKR. Not new. Not trying to be new. Something better — a modern classic that had earned its lines instead of borrowing them from a focus group. Long, low, curved, wood-lined, supercharged, and completely uninterested in whatever app-based appliance was humming beside it at the lights.

That was the rabbit hole. And if you follow the Jaguar rabbit hole far enough, you eventually find yourself staring at the Mark II.

At first glance it should not be the one that gets you. It is a four-door saloon from another Britain, all picnic-era manners, chrome, wood and upright dignity — the sort of car a man in a hat might step out of carrying documents. But the longer you look, the more dangerous it becomes. The proportions tighten. The stance lowers. The glasshouse glows. The little haunch over the rear wheel starts to speak. The grille stops looking polite and starts looking hungry.

And then you learn what it could do. That is when the Mark II stops being an old saloon and becomes something else entirely. A gentleman's club with a cosh under the seat.

The Missing Bloodline

This is also why the Mark II feels more important now.

For decades, Jaguar kept returning to this idea: the beautiful, fast saloon. The XJ. The XJR. The later XF performance models. Cars that understood speed could wear a suit — that you did not have to look German to be taken seriously.

But that bloodline has gone quiet. The XJ ended in 2019. The XE, XF and F-Type all bowed out in mid-2024. And in December 2025, the last combustion Jaguar of all — the F-Pace — rolled off the line at Solihull and was handed to the heritage collection, leaving the marque to fall silent altogether while it reinvents itself, all-electric, for a 2026 relaunch.

That makes the Mark II feel less like an old curiosity and more like an origin myth. It was not just the beginning of Jaguar's fast-saloon story. It may be one of the purest expressions of it.

Before screens.
Before drive modes.
Before performance became a software package.
Before luxury cabins became black plastic caves with tablets glued to the dash.

The Mark II was speed with smell, wood, chrome, glass, danger and charm. It was not trying to be the future. It accidentally helped invent one.

The Dark Side

Maybe that is why the Mark II refuses to become merely pretty. It has too much shadow for that.

Mark Twain wrote that everyone is a moon, with a dark side he never shows to anybody. The Mark II may be the automotive version of that line.

One side faces the world: polished walnut, Connolly leather, chrome, grace — a doctor's saloon, an executive's reward, a motor car fit for dinner at a proper hotel. But slip around behind it and there is the other side: touring-car aggression, police pursuit, bank work, wet-road oversteer, and a straight-six that could turn a respectable man into a very fast liar.

That is the secret. The Mark II is not beautiful because it is innocent. It is beautiful because it is not.

Bottom Line: The Suit, the Knife, and the Smile

The Jaguar Mark II was not merely a beautiful old saloon.

It was one of the original fast luxury sedans. It was a genuine performance car with four doors. It was a racing saloon. It was a police car. It was a bank robber's tool. It was an executive express. It was a family car capable of humiliating sports cars. It was respectable enough for the driveway and quick enough for the getaway.

That is why it is special. Not because it was flawless. Not because every story is clean. Not because nostalgia has polished away the truth. It is special because it contained contradiction better than almost any car of its era.

It had grace, but it was not soft.
It had space, but it was not dull.
It had pace, but it did not shout.
It had elegance — but there was always something in the pocket.

A beautifully tailored suit? Yes. But with a knife in the pocket, a racing licence in the glovebox, and the police using the very same car behind you.

That is the Jaguar Mark II.
